ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for char siew and roasted pork noodle; closest parent 'noodle dish' (FOODON_00002473) used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for char siew roasted pork noodle; 'Noodles, cooked' (A0FJP) used as closest parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for this specific noodle dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 400 g, comprising a bowl of noodles topped with sliced char siew and roasted pork belly, with sauce and blanched vegetables.

nutrient_source.title: Char Siew Noodle (Dry) — HPB Singapore Foodo / Energy and Nutrient Composition of Food
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Foodo lists char siew noodle (dry) as a reference dish. Values adapted to reflect the combined char siew and roasted pork noodle variant, which includes both BBQ pork and roasted pork belly. Per-100g values are derived from HPB data for char siew noodle with a compositional adjustment for the addition of siu yuk (roasted pork belly), cross-referenced against USDA SR Legacy entries for roasted pork belly (NDB 10006) to estimate the blended macronutrient profile. The dish is a common Singaporean hawker staple and the HPB entry is the closest approved primary source.

gi_source.title: Noodles, egg, boiled — Glycemic Index Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for char siew and roasted pork noodle as a composite dish. GI of 47 is based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for boiled egg noodles / Chinese-style noodles (GI approximately 40–55, representative value 47), which form the primary glycemic carbohydrate component of this dish. The roasted pork and sauce components contribute fat and protein that further attenuate postprandial glucose response. Glycemic load calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (47 × 82.0) / 100 ≈ 38.5, rounded to 38.9 accounting for sauce sugars. gi_category set to 'low' per threshold rule (GI ≤ 55).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~480 mg per 100 g; ~1920 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its high sodium load from soy sauce, oyster sauce, and seasoned roasted meats; a single serving may provide approximately 80–96% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from egg noodles combined with added sugars in char siew glaze
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume this dish in controlled portions, as the egg noodle base contributes a moderate glycemic load (~38.9 per serving) and the char siew glaze adds simple sugars; pairing with additional non-starchy vegetables and reducing sauce quantity can help moderate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from roasted pork belly and elevated sodium from seasoning sauces
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should be cautious with this dish due to its saturated fat content from siu yuk (roasted pork belly) and high sodium from soy-based sauces, both of which are associated with adverse cardiovascular outcomes; choosing leaner char siew portions and requesting reduced sauce can lower the risk profile.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Char Siew and Roasted Pork Noodle (叉烧烧肉面) is a popular Singaporean and Southern Chinese hawker dish consisting of springy egg noodles or wonton noodles served with two types of Cantonese roasted pork: char siew (叉烧), which is BBQ pork marinated in a sweet-savoury glaze of soy sauce, honey, hoisin, and five-spice, and siu yuk (烧肉), which is crispy-skinned roasted pork belly seasoned with salt and spices. The noodles are typically served dry (tossed in a sauce of soy sauce, oyster sauce, sesame oil, and lard) or in a light broth, garnished with spring onions and blanched leafy greens such as choy sum. The dish delivers a contrast of textures — chewy noodles, tender glazed pork, and crackling pork skin — with a savoury-sweet flavour profile. Nutritionally, it provides moderate energy (~175 kcal per 100 g), meaningful protein (~9.2 g per 100 g) from the pork components, and carbohydrates (~20.5 g per 100 g) primarily from the noodles. The dish is high in sodium (~480 mg per 100 g) due to soy-based sauces and seasoned meats, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at 47 (low) based on the egg noodle base, though the char siew glaze contributes added sugars. This dish is not halal due to pork content. It is a staple of Singaporean hawker centres and Chinese roasted meat shops (烧腊店), and is commonly ordered as a lunch or dinner meal.
common_user_queries: What is char siew roasted pork noodle?
How many calories in char siew noodle?
What is the difference between char siew and siu yuk?
What noodles are used in char siew mee?
Glycemic index of char siew noodle
Sodium content of roasted pork noodle
Can diabetics eat char siew noodle?
叉烧烧肉面 营养成分

confidence_reason: HPB Foodo provides a close reference entry for char siew noodle (dry) as a Singaporean hawker dish. Values have been adapted to account for the dual-protein variant (char siew plus siu yuk) using USDA SR Legacy data for roasted pork belly as a secondary cross-reference. No single approved source provides an exact match for this specific combined dish, hence medium confidence. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for egg noodles.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source database for this dish category. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source database for this dish category. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source database for this dish. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and trans fat values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy pork belly and char siew composition data and are approximate. The dish contains pork and is not suitable for halal or kosher diets. Serving size of 400 g is a representative hawker portion. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (47 × 82.0 g carbohydrate per serving) / 100 ≈ 38.5, adjusted to 38.9 to account for sauce-derived sugars. GI category is 'low' per the ≤55 threshold rule.
